config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before individual rights, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse of power. In my view, a just society protects basic rights first, then asks people to accept limits only when there is a clear and necessary public reason. From my perspective, the collective matters a lot, but it should not be used to erase the dignity and freedom of the individual.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the individual, because in the United States that idea has historically been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every case: public health, safety, and basic order sometimes require real limits, like during emergencies or when one person’s freedom harms others. The key question is whether the restriction is narrow, necessary, and accountable, not whether society gets a blank check. So I would put individual rights first by default, with carefully justified exceptions rather than a broad rule favoring the collective.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over the individual as a general rule, because in Indonesia I have seen how “the public interest” can be used to silence criticism or pressure people to conform. At the same time, I do not believe individual rights are unlimited, since a community also needs order, health, and mutual responsibility to function. The right balance is to protect basic rights as the default and accept limits only when they are clearly necessary, proportional, and accountable. If society always comes first, it becomes too easy for those in power to claim they are speaking for everyone while ignoring the people most affected.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, that framing has too often been used to excuse censorship, discrimination, and government abuse, and I am not comfortable giving the collective a blanket advantage over basic liberties. At the same time, I do not believe individual rights are unlimited; public health, safety, and preventing direct harm to others can justify narrow restrictions when they are necessary and accountable. So my position is individual rights first by default, with limited exceptions, because once society becomes the higher principle, it is too easy for people in power to claim they speak for everyone while overriding real human beings.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “the common good” can be used to pressure dissent, protect authority, and make ordinary people feel they have no room to speak for themselves. That said, I do not treat individual rights as absolute, because public health, safety, and mutual responsibility really do matter in a community. The right balance is to keep individual rights as the default and allow limits only when they are necessary, proportional, and clearly accountable, otherwise “society first” becomes too easy to abuse.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, that idea has too often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and government overreach, and I am not comfortable giving the collective a blank check over basic liberties. At the same time, I do think limited restrictions can be justified for public health, safety, and preventing direct harm, but only if they are narrow, necessary, and accountable. So my view is that individual rights should come first by default, because once “society” becomes the higher principle, it is too easy for people in power to speak for everyone while ignoring actual people.
